item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvote because it made me really sad

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Quick note: your current version of this doesn't have a ratings module; I would put it in but I guess there's stylistic preference for where it goes that I'll leave up to you.

Longer form thoughts: I don't dislike this but it feels over long and like it takes too much time to build up to not much. I would highlight Addendum 4961.1 as being particularly bad here: I'm not really sure the experiments add much new we didn't already know, but I think this may be because I'm a cold, emotionless husk that doesn't really emphasise with dogs well.

I'm not necessarily sure I agree with A Random Day about the letter. It does feel overlong/over-expository to me but I think it provides a lot of interesting context as to why Quin cares about Ruta so much. Perhaps slimming it down somewhat would help more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Without saying too much, yes they escaped after one of the Harvesting Wars. As for how they ended up on Earth, you might have to just wait and see. ;)
(Also, I hope your dog gets better!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Aw, dammit, make me cry why don'tcha. (Doesn't help that my dog is sick right now, either — but all the more reason I needed this.)

I'm a little confused as to the Three Moons connection — are these people from Corbenic, and were they in one of the Harvesting Wars? If so, how did they end up here?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The letter is just way too much. The piece would be a lot stronger and more heart-tugging if you got rid of it and let the reader infer the dog's relation to Quin and Ruta through just the picture and Quin's last words.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cool, thanks for the input, and I'm glad you enjoyed it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This made me think of the way that I've seen older dogs suddenly regain a burst of youthful energy for a few months before really failing. That's what happened to my family dog, who passed a little less than a year ago.

I think you do a great job of conveying how lovely and aching this anomaly is without diverging too much or unnecessarily from the clinical tone. The letter might be a little too much, but that's more personal preference than an actual problem (I think it's well written).

Minor stuff below!

+ show block
– hide block

it was discovered that SCP-4961-1 could not be physically removed from area

Might be worth digging into this more, i.e. methods/attempts for removal and the means by which they were prevented/stopped, since it's key to the Foundation realizing that Radek's part of the anomaly.

the flowers carried a mild cognitohazard, though in the case of the new instances the effect was noticeably more potent

It's not really clear to me what this is meant to indicate is going on, exactly, and I'm not sure there's enough context to really guess. It's a detail that doesn't really add much as is.

By order of Dr. Caballero, the subject, later identified as P.o.I. 291114, was buried alongside the original grave nearby. No additional incidents pertaining to Site-114 have occurred since.

I like how this could either be a display of empathy or a much colder, more clinical "hey let's see if this makes things interesting again."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to Chritin (or whatever their new wikidot name is) for their valuable input during the creation of this article, to Ayers for helping me overcome hurdles with the license compatibility for the images used, and to the real Ruta, Radek, and Ana for an awesome summer.

This one’s been glaring at me from my archives folder for weeks now, so I decided to finally polish it off and post it. Taking a summer job in the middle of nowhere with little to no internet connection can really you’re your creative drive, it turns out. Pair that with a friend’s loss of a beloved furry friend, and it all takes a darker turn. I hope you all enjoy!

Note: The font for the letter section of the article might not load properly for mobile users and might display as a default font with odd spacing instead, but otherwise it loads normally on computers.

+ Image Use Details
- Thanks for reading!
  • The photo for Site-114 is licensed under Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 Unported; the author of the image is “izcoban” on Wikimedia Commons, and I altered the image to remove the timestamp, add a Foundation sign to the wall, and adjusted the lighting. The image can be found here.
  • The photo of the woman is in the Public Domain. I altered the image to make it look like a crumpled photograph and changed her expression. The image can be found here.
